title: Cognitive Distortion Spotter
displayTitle: Eleven Thinking Traps. Find the Two Costing You the Most.
tagline: A four-minute scan of how your head narrates your day — including how it narrates AI.

children.text: Twelve scenarios. Each gives you four possible internal monologues. Pick the one closest to what your head actually does — not the one that sounds best. The result picks up patterns; the patterns are useful regardless of where you land.
Eleven distortion families, including one — AI-relative distortion — that's specific to the current moment.

questions.prompt: You gave a strategic presentation to your senior team. Two people seemed engaged. Four were on their phones. What does your head do?

options.label: That was a disaster.
They obviously thought it was terrible.
The phones don't matter — what matters is the two engaged people.
I'm just not good at presenting.

questions.prompt: You're drafting a strategy memo for the board. You've written one version yourself, and one using AI as a thinking partner. The AI version is more articulate but says some things you're not sure you actually believe.

options.label: The AI version is better written so I should go with it.
Anything AI touches is suspect — I should throw it out.
Good language, but the substance has to be mine. Use the structure, rewrite the conviction.
If I use the AI version and it goes well, am I really the strategist?

questions.prompt: Your board chair gives you feedback: three strong positives, one area for development.

options.label: So I'm failing at that one thing.
The positives are just being polite. The criticism is the real message.
Three things going well, one to work on. Fair.
I should have been better. I should have caught that myself.

questions.prompt: You're deciding whether to restructure a function in your remit using AI tooling, which would mean a significant headcount reduction.

options.label: It's my fault these roles will go. I should have built a different team.
If I don't recommend the AI option, the board will see me as out of touch.
The vendor's analysis is detailed and convincing — I should follow their recommendation.
Real decision: trade-offs, named, owned. Including the human ones.

questions.prompt: You sent the wrong version of a strategic document to a key external stakeholder.

options.label: I'm so unprofessional.
This is going to lose us the relationship.
Apologise, send the right version, learn the lesson, move on.
I always do this.

questions.prompt: A peer recommends a course of action. They've clearly used AI to develop their analysis. The recommendation sounds confident and comprehensive.

options.label: If they've used AI and the output is this thorough, it's probably right.
AI-generated thinking has no real authority. I'll discount it.
The analysis looks thorough — let me check the assumptions before I accept the recommendation.
If I question this they'll think I don't understand AI.

questions.prompt: Two of your direct reports are at odds over which AI tooling to adopt. You haven't yet intervened.

options.label: I feel anxious about it, so it must be serious.
If I don't fix this, the whole team will fall apart.
They're adults. Some friction is normal. Watch it, intervene if needed.
A good leader would have prevented this by setting clearer tooling direction earlier.

questions.prompt: You've been asked to lead something significantly bigger than your current scope — and AI is heavily implicated in how it'll be delivered.

options.label: There's no way I'm ready.
If I'm not perfect at it, I've failed.
Stretching. Probably not comfortable. Worth doing.
I'm not technical enough — they'll find out.

questions.prompt: You're working on a strategic recommendation. You ask an AI tool for its view on the same question. It comes back with a different answer than yours, well-argued.

options.label: If the AI disagrees with me, I'm probably wrong.
An AI can't really understand strategy — disregard.
Worth understanding where we differ. The disagreement might point to something I haven't considered.
If I go with my view and I'm wrong, I'll look like I ignored the AI.

questions.prompt: A promotion you were hoping for went to someone else. They're known for being aggressive with AI tooling.

options.label: It's my fault. I should have pushed harder on the AI agenda.
I'll never get promoted at this rate — I'm too analogue.
It feels like a rejection of who I am as a leader.
One decision. Many factors. Let me find out what actually drove the call.

questions.prompt: An AI vendor pitches you a tool that will, they claim, transform how your function operates. The demo is impressive.

options.label: The demo was impressive — we should commit.
AI vendors always over-promise. Decline.
Impressive demos are designed to be impressive. The real question is what happens in week 12.
If I don't move on this and a competitor does, I'm done.

questions.prompt: A significant initiative has failed. Several factors contributed, including some of yours, and including an AI tool that produced a confident but ultimately wrong recommendation.

options.label: This is entirely on me — I shouldn't have trusted the AI.
I should have seen the AI's output for what it was.
Multiple causes. Including my judgement on when to trust the model. Worth understanding all of them.
Everything I touch with AI goes wrong.

children.text: Your default patterns are healthier than most senior leaders'. The work isn't correction — it's maintenance. Show this to anyone you coach or manage.
Your two strongest distortion patterns are below. Worth sitting with — not because they're catastrophic, but because they're shaping decisions you don't realise you're making.
Your default thinking is reaching for distortions more often than it's reaching for clear reads. That's not a verdict — it's a pattern, and patterns are workable. The top two are below. Start there.

You answered most scenarios with the healthy option. Worth being explicit about what that looks like in your day. The patterns aren't absent — they show up under pressure, when tired, when stakes are high. The work is noticing when you slip into them, not eliminating them. Read the heatmap for the distortions you do reach for occasionally; that's where the small leverage lives.

Sounds like: "That was a disaster." "I always do this." "Either I'm good at it or I'm useless." You lose the middle, which is where most of leadership lives. Your team feels the binary in your reactions — wins are wins, but stumbles register as catastrophes — and they start curating what they bring you. Empirical: what evidence is there that there isn't a middle here? Logical: does one bad instance actually make everything bad? Pragmatic: does thinking this way help you do better next time, or worse?

Sounds like: "The one negative comment is what matters. The praise was just politeness." You discount your wins and amplify your losses, which produces an accurate-feeling but distorted view of your own performance and your team's. Empirical: if a peer described their quarter this way, would you accept their version? Logical: why does the negative get more weight than the positive? Pragmatic: where is this getting you?

Sounds like: "It wasn't really that good." "Anyone could have done it." Confidence stops compounding. Your team mirrors your low evaluation of their work. Boards lose the narrative of progress. Empirical: if someone else had delivered what you did, would you describe it the same way? Logical: why are you the exception to your own standards? Pragmatic: what would change if you allowed the win to be a win?

Sounds like: "It's my fault." "I should have caught it." "I'm responsible for the whole thing." Energy that should be going to the next move goes to self-flagellation about the last one. You model this for your team, who learn either to mirror it or to walk on eggshells. Empirical: what were the other factors? Name them. Logical: does owning your share require you to own everyone's share? Pragmatic: what does this story do for you that a more balanced one wouldn't?

Sounds like: "I feel anxious about it, so it must be serious." "I feel guilty, so I must have done something wrong." Feelings become evidence for themselves. You can't get out of a feeling using its own logic — and at senior level you tend not to have the people around you who'll question it. Empirical: what evidence is there for this, separate from how you feel? Logical: does feeling something make it true? Pragmatic: what would you do if the feeling weren't there?

Sounds like: "They obviously thought it was bad." "She's avoiding me." "The board has lost confidence." You make calls based on what you've decided others are thinking, which is almost never accurate, and which they're not getting to defend. Empirical: what evidence do you have for what they're thinking, beyond assumption? Logical: if you can't reliably read your own mind, why are you reliably reading theirs? Pragmatic: what would you do if you asked them directly?

Sounds like: "This is going to fail." "I'll never recover from this." "The board will say no." You make decisions to avoid outcomes you've predicted into existence. Predicted failures prevent the attempts that would have shown the prediction was wrong. Empirical: how often do your predictions actually turn out right? Logical: does past difficulty guarantee future failure? Pragmatic: if you treated the prediction as a hypothesis instead of a fact, what would you do differently?

Sounds like: "I'm bad at this." "I'm an imposter." "I'm not a technical leader." You turn one behaviour into a permanent identity. The identity then justifies more of the behaviour. The loop closes — and at senior level it shapes your career trajectory. Empirical: where is the evidence that this is who you are rather than what you did once? Logical: if a colleague had done what you did, would you label them this way? Pragmatic: what's available if you describe the behaviour instead of yourself?

Sounds like: "I should have known better." "A real leader would have caught this." "I must be across all of it." A rigid internal rulebook that nobody can live up to, including you. Shoulds produce shame, not change. Empirical: whose rule is this? Did you choose it, or inherit it? Logical: does "should" make it more likely you'll do it next time, or less? Pragmatic: try "could" instead. What changes?

Sounds like: "This is the end of the relationship." "I'll lose my reputation over this." "I won't be able to handle it." You skip from the current problem to the worst possible outcome, then live with the dread of that outcome as if it had already happened. Your team senses the dread without knowing what's driving it. Empirical: what's the most likely outcome, not the worst-case one? Logical: even if the worst happened, is it true you couldn't cope? Pragmatic: what's available if you assume you can handle whatever comes?

Sounds like: "If the AI says so, it's probably right." "The output looks comprehensive, so it must be." "I shouldn't disagree with the model — it's seen more data than I have." You're outsourcing judgement to a tool that produces confident output regardless of whether the underlying call is sound. Senior judgement is exactly the thing AI is least equipped to substitute for — it's where the irreducible human contribution lives — and over-trusting AI here is the most expensive version of this distortion. Empirical: when you've checked the AI's reasoning carefully, how often has it been quietly wrong on the parts that matter? Logical: confident output and correct output are different things. Why would the model's confidence be evidence of its correctness? Pragmatic: what would you do if you treated AI output as a peer's draft — useful, worth reading, definitely not final?

Sounds like: "AI can't really do strategy." "Anything AI touches is suspect." "The model doesn't understand my context." You're refusing input that your peers are increasingly incorporating into how they decide. This is sometimes principled (you've done the work and concluded the tool isn't useful for your case) and sometimes inertial (you haven't done the work). The cost depends on which. Empirical: when was the last time you actually tested whether AI was useful on a specific decision you faced — not in the abstract, but on a real one? Logical: "can't do strategy" and "can't do this particular strategic question" are different claims. Which one are you actually making? Pragmatic: what would change if you treated AI as a competent intern? Worth checking; not in charge.
Want your top two distortions and a thoughts-record worksheet you can use this week?
Or book a 30-minute conversation about the pattern that surfaced →

seoDescription: Eleven thinking traps senior leaders reach for, including AI-relative distortions. Find the two costing you the most. Four minutes.
